feedback.text: Hello, I write to you as a. Permanent resident of the Netherlands. I am submitting this feedback concerning the urgent need for enhanced consumer protections within the digital marketplace, specifically regarding the practice of video game publishers terminating support for and rendering purchased games unplayable. This issue should be addressed at the European Union level, potentially through a dedicated Digital Fairness Act or as a significant amendment to existing consumer protection directives. The EU is the appropriate body to legislate this matter, as it can establish a unified standard for the entire single market, preventing companies from exploiting legal loopholes in individual member states. The current situation, where a consumer can purchase a digital product, only for the publisher to unilaterally revoke access and functionality at a later date, is fundamentally unjust. This practice undermines the very concept of ownership. As a consumer, my expectation is that a product I pay for remains usable. I currently invest my time and money in games like Elite Dangerous, Warframe, and Star Citizen, living with the uncertainty that these digital worlds and my progress within them could be erased at the publisher's discretion. This is not a hypothetical concern. I have personally lost access to games I purchased and enjoyed. The list of killed games grows each year and includes titles like The Crew, The Cycle: Frontier, Knockout City, Rumbleverse, Apex Legends Mobile, and Babylon's Fall. In each case, the publisher's decision to shut down servers meant the product ceased to exist for the consumer, with no recourse or remedy offered. Therefore, I urge the European Commission to propose, and the Parliament to enact, legislation with the following specific legal requirements: 1. Mandate of Perpetual Functionality: For any game sold for a one-time price, the publisher must be legally obligated to ensure consumers have the option to keep the game playable in the state it was sold. This can be fulfilled through a one-time commitment at the end of the game's official support lifecycle. This commitment would involve preparing and releasing the necessary tools for the community to host their own private servers. Once this option is provided, the developer or publisher will bear no further costs or ongoing fees to keep the game running. The core principle is simply granting consumers the option to preserve the game themselves. Whether the community chooses to act on this option or not, the publisher's financial obligation is concluded. This ensures no impact on profit margins from ongoing support costs and places the future of the game in the hands of its players. 2. End-of-Life Server Obligation: If a publisher decides to cease official online support for a game, they must be required to release the necessary server-side software or provide a patch that enables peer-to-peer hosting or a fully functional offline mode. This would allow consumer-run communities to preserve the game, respecting both the publisher's right to end official support and the consumer's right to use their purchased product. 3. Clarity in Advertising: Games that require a persistent online connection to function must be explicitly labeled as online-only services at the point of sale, not as a simple product purchase. The terms of service must not be allowed to supersede the consumer's statutory rights to a functional product. Action from the European Union is the only viable path to correct this imbalance of power and ensure that when consumers in Europe buy a game, they are truly buying a product, not merely a temporary license to play at the whim of a corporation.
